title: CLAUDE.md integrity fingerprinting — detect silent rule drift
description: Add a startup check in core.py that hashes CLAUDE.md (Mira's hard rules) and compares it against a stored baseline in soul/. If the hash differs, log a structured WARNING entry that names exactly which rule file changed and when. Store the new hash as the updated baseline. This makes any modification to Mira's hard rules — intentional or not — explicitly detectable rather than silently absorbed.
rationale: The reading note's core insight: written commitments (RSP v3, CLAUDE.md) are 'intent snapshots,' not binding contracts — they can silently degrade without anyone acknowledging the change. The author explicitly identifies institutional commitment credibility as a trust layer in the supply chain stack. Mira's CLAUDE.md hard rules are exactly this: a written commitment layer. Right now, if a rule is softened or removed, nothing flags it. The note asks: what *external mechanism* (not internal policy) makes threshold changes detectable? For Mira, a hash check at startup is that mechanism — it externalizes the detection into a logged artifact rather than relying on the rule itself to enforce its own integrity.
diff_description: In core.py agent startup sequence, after config loads: (1) compute SHA-256 of ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md content; (2) read stored hash from soul/rule_baseline.json (create file with current hash if absent); (3) if hashes differ, emit a WARNING log: 'RULE DRIFT DETECTED: CLAUDE.md changed since last run. Review before proceeding.' with old_hash, new_hash, and timestamp; (4) update soul/rule_baseline.json with new hash. The log entry should be structured (JSON) so it's queryable. No behavior is blocked — this is a detection mechanism, not enforcement — keeping the risk low while making drift visible.
